TextENGNew: While I waited, I kept watch over the school grounds, but nothing seemed out of the ordinary.
Gazing at the elegantly illuminated Western-style architecture, it became harder and harder to believe this place was actually a school.
...If it's going to function as a detention facility, it should at least look the part -- electrified wire, searchlights, Dobermans and all.
changeReason: New correctly identifies 収容施設 as "detention facility" (not "prison") and specifies "Dobermans" matching the Japanese ドーベルマン, whereas old used generic "guard dogs."

TextENGNew: I'd expected that a moment like this would bring a rush of school memories flashing before my eyes,@k but nothing of the sort happened.
That kind of nostalgic montage only works when you actually have fond memories to recall.
I hadn't had a single pleasant experience at this academy, so there was simply nothing for the reel to play.
...If I absolutely had to compile a highlight reel of good memories, it would probably be nothing but the lavish dinners.
In that case, my life-flashing-before-my-eyes would consist entirely of Japanese cuisine, Western dishes, Chinese food, and French gastronomy parading past.
...What kind of farewell montage is that?
changeReason: Old translates as a generic exclamation ("What the hell?") while new ties it to the preceding montage discussion, better matching the contextual meaning of なんだそりゃ.

TextENGNew: The image was so absurd it caught me off guard.@k I burst out laughing.
...Just then, I heard the sound of a car approaching.
I crouched back into the darkness.
The license plate matched the one I'd been told to expect.@k ...No mistake.
I sprang from my hiding spot, dashed to the passenger side of the slowly moving car, wrenched the door open, and threw myself inside.
``Well done on your escape, ma'am.@k@vS06/06/150600004. Shion-san.``
Behind the wheel sat a man of early old age.
...He'd take offense if he heard me say that, though.@k He still fancies himself middle-aged.
Seeing that I was safe and reading the triumph on my face, he broke into a grin.
``Ahh, fresh air at last~!@k@vS06/06/150600004. Right now, I'd kill for a bowl of plain white rice!``
changeReason: Old incorrectly translates 銀シャリ (polished white rice) as "burger"; new correctly renders it as "plain white rice."

TextENGNew: ``Ahahahaha!@k@vS06/15/151500004. ...I'd heard the cuisine at that academy was rather exquisite.@k@vS06/15/151500005. Were they serving you nothing but prison gruel, Shion-san?``
``Don't be stupid, of course not!@k@vS06/06/150600006. Obviously I was kidding.@k@vS06/06/150600007. Come on, if you've got time to laugh, step on the gas!``
``Yes, yes. As you command...``
Kasai didn't bother hiding his amusement, laughing heartily. After reminding me to buckle my seatbelt, he floored the accelerator.
The academy, elegant as a European cathedral, vanished from the rearview mirror in an instant.
It disappeared so unceremoniously that I never got the chance to watch my montage of exquisite international dinners flash before my eyes.
Farewell, my beloved school days!
Just you watch -- starting today, I'm going to fill this refined stomach of mine with the trashiest junk food I can find.
I'll lead a life forever free of the table manners you drilled into me!@k Ahahaha!@k Serves you right!
The chic atmosphere -- so convincing you could almost mistake it for a corner of Europe --@k soon gave way to vast farmland and dreary streetlights as we drove.
At last, I could remember that this was just the backwaters of Japan.
``Are you hungry?@k@vS06/15/151500008. Shall we stop somewhere to eat?``
``I'm fine -- I already ate.``
That was a small lie.
The truth was, I was a bit hungry.
I'd barely touched my dinner, not wanting a full stomach to slow me down during the escape.
Kasai's offer was tempting, but I couldn't bring myself to sit and leisurely enjoy a meal while still this close to enemy territory.
``At this hour, if we get on the highway, the service areas will only have vending machines...@k@vS06/15/151500010. ...Though I suppose there are hamburger vending machines these days.``
``Oh, and speaking of which -- I've heard they even have oden vending machines now.``
changeReason: Old fragment "and oden vending machines nowadays" was split from previous entry; new correctly makes this a standalone remark about oden vending machines, matching the Japanese.

TextENGNew: ``Huh?!@k@vS06/06/150600010. What the hell?@k@vS06/06/150600011. Does a cup pop out and then the ingredients just plop down into it, like a coffee vending machine...?@k@vS06/06/150600012. That's disgusting~~~``
``Ahaha, apparently it's not like that.@k@vS06/15/151500013. It comes in a can, I hear -- the whole thing just rolls out.``
``...Ah, sorry.@k@vS06/06/150600014. I'll pass on anything canned.``
``Still can't do canned food...?@k@vS06/15/151500015. I'd have thought you'd outgrown that by now.``
``...What I hate, I hate.@k@vS06/06/150600016. Oh, shut up.@k@vS06/06/150600017. What are you laughing at?!``
Kasai chuckled softly to himself.
He was probably chattering on like this to help me unwind, I thought.
...Kasai wasn't normally this talkative.
``I'm wiped out.@k@vS06/06/150600019. Let me get some sleep.@k@vS06/06/150600020. ...How do I recline this seat?``
``Try feeling around the base on your left side.@k@vS06/15/151500017. Is there a lever there?``
``Mm, found it, found it.@k@vS06/06/150600022. ...Hup we go.``
Kasai laughed cheerfully again.
``Did I do something funny?``
``No, no.@k@vS06/15/151500019. ...It's just that 'hup we go' -- that old-lady phrasing reminded me of Mion-san.``
...Well, naturally.
Mion and I are identical twins.@k We're perfect copies of each other.
If I react to something a certain way, Mion would react the same.
If Mion reacted to something in a given situation, put me in the same spot and I'd do the exact same thing.
``How's Sis doing?``
``She seemed well, last I checked.@k@vS06/15/151500021. I only catch a glimpse of her when I bring your father to the family councils.``
``Right, Sis doesn't live at home anymore.@k@vS06/06/150600026. She's at Granny's place in Hinamizawa now, isn't she?``
``She moved into the Sonozaki main house around the same time you enrolled at the academy.``
...I'd asked the question myself, yet I responded with a disinterested 'hmm.'
The 'Sonozaki main house' is, to put it simply, the residence of Sonozaki Oryou -- the current head of the Sonozaki family.
In other words, our grandmother's house.
...That said, when people use the phrase 'the main house,' it usually carries meaning far beyond a mere dwelling.
changeReason: Old adds "who I call a hag" not present in the Japanese (つまり祖母の実家のことを指す simply means "our grandmother's house"); new is a more accurate translation.

TextENGNew: And the way Kasai used it just now was no exception.
``...Has she changed at all?``
``You mean Mion-san?``
``Yeah.@k@vS06/06/150600029. ...Living under the same roof as that old hag morning to night -- she must've changed at least a little.``
Kasai let out another suppressed laugh.
He knows perfectly well how I feel about our grandmother.
``As far as I can tell, she hasn't changed.@k@vS06/15/151500025. ...She's the same Mion-san you've always known.``
``What about me?@k@vS06/06/150600031. ...Have I changed?``
``No.@k@vS06/15/151500027. Not in the slightest.``
Kasai answered instantly, a mischievous smile playing on his lips.
...We hadn't seen each other in ages, and yet he answered without a moment's hesitation. Somehow that annoyed me.
When he noticed me glaring at him with a sulky pout, Kasai could no longer contain himself and erupted into laughter.

``Ahahahaha!@k@vS06/15/151500029. You haven't changed one bit.@k@vS06/15/151500030. Considering you spent all that time in such a prestigious academy, it's genuinely impressive how utterly unchanged you are.@k@vS06/15/151500031. Heh heh heh heh...!``
``Hmph~~.@k@vS06/06/150600033. Laugh all you want!@k@vS06/06/150600034. I'm going to sleep -- I mean it!``
``There's a blanket in the backseat.@k@vS06/15/151500033. Please use it.``
``Mm, thanks.``
I snatched the blanket and wrapped myself up in it.
Despite how exhausted I'd felt, the moment I actually settled into a sleeping position, drowsiness stubbornly refused to come. Strange.
No, not really.
My body and mind must both be wrung out from the unfamiliar ordeal of the escape.
If I couldn't feel the fatigue, it was only because the tension hadn't faded yet.
So I said it aloud one more time, commanding myself to sleep while I still could.
``I'm sleeping!@k@vS06/06/150600037. I'm seriously going to sleep now!``
``Yes, yes -- that's the third time you've announced it.@k@vS06/15/151500035. Do you need my permission before you can nod off?``
``You're way too chatty tonight!@k@vS06/06/150600039. I can't get drowsy if you don't pipe down for a minute!``
``My sincerest apologies, my lady.@k@vS06/15/151500037. I shall hold my tongue for a while.@k@vS06/15/151500038. ...Heh heh heh...!``
This idle banter with Kasai took me back to the days when I'd still been living an ordinary life in Okinomiya.
Though 'ordinary' might not be the right word for it.
We'd been in the thick of the Dam War back then, with chaos from morning till night.
Everyone banded together, and looking back now, we pulled off some seriously dangerous stunts.
If any of it were dug up piece by piece, I'd be looking at juvenile hall for sure.@k ...Actually, half the population of Hinamizawa would probably end up behind bars.
But we never thought of what we did as crimes.
It was a war.
It had the trappings of war, but at the same time, it may also have been a kind of festival.
A festival where every person with ties to Hinamizawa stood united against a common enemy.
It was a fierce struggle, but looking back on it now, it was exhilarating in its own way.
Pelting the riot police with stones alongside everyone else -- that was probably going too far, I'll admit.
We'd bolt from the pursuing officers and duck into someone's house for shelter.
We'd swarm the police station, demanding the release of arrested comrades.
Sneaking into the construction site to carry out various acts of sabotage felt less like actual warfare and more like playing at war.
My heart pounded. My blood raced.
...The sense of solidarity with fellow fighters whose faces I didn't even know -- even now, remembering it warms my chest.

Yes, that feeling was... like everyone hauling a festival palanquin through the village together, drenched in sweat and utterly spent.@k ...And at the end, we'd all collapse flat on the ground, strangers splashing barley tea on one another in giddy celebration.
I was still just a brat back then.
I look back fondly on all the trouble I got into, tagging along with the young guys who came and went from my father's place.
I got picked up by the police more than a few times, too.
But honestly, it didn't feel much different from being called to the teachers' office for forgetting my homework.
...The grown-ups were murderous with rage over their homes being drowned under a reservoir,@k but for us kids, looking back, those were actually pretty fun times.
That lively Dam War came to an abrupt end a few years ago.
It wasn't just the surface-level, festival-like activism that brought the dam project to a halt.
Behind the scenes, the Sonozaki main house and my father's people had been working in the shadows, pulling strings.
...This is a closely guarded Sonozaki family secret, but...@k rumor has it they may or may not have kidnapped the grandson of the Minister of Construction and used him as leverage.
The grandson turned up a few days after the abduction.
...He was found deep in the mountains near Yagouchi.
He didn't just vanish into thin air -- he wasn't spirited away.@k He was 'discovered.'@k Which means...@k some kind of deal was struck behind closed doors.
The following year, the dam project declared an indefinite freeze on construction and was effectively dead.
Based on the grandson's testimony, the police suspected several Hinamizawa residents were directly involved and investigated relentlessly -- but it was futile.@k They couldn't turn up a single lead.
changeReason: Old omits that police specifically suspected "several Hinamizawa residents of direct involvement" (住民数人が直接的に関与) and their persistent investigation (しつこく), condensing significant detail.

TextENGNew: The comrades of the festival would never betray one of their own.
To protect their people, they'd do anything -- tell any lie.
...Alibis, circumstantial evidence -- all fabricated with practiced ease.
...As if the police could ever uncover the truth.@k ...Ha!
The car lurched sharply, probably from rough pavement, and I snapped back to reality.
...And I noticed that I'd been wearing an absurdly cheerful expression.
I'm actually looking forward to going home.
``Shion-san.@k@vS06/15/151500040. ...Are you still awake?``
``About where to take you... you'd like to go to Okinomiya, correct?``
``Where else would I go?``
I shot back irritably, as if he'd asked whether one plus one equals two.
changeReason: Old omits the 1+1=2 analogy (当り前なことを聞くな = "don't ask such obvious things") present in the Japanese, simply saying "answered straightforwardly with a frown."

TextENGNew: And Kasai, in turn, responded just as matter-of-factly.

``The decision to enroll you at that boarding academy was made by the head of the Sonozaki family.@k@vS06/15/151500044. ...You've now fled from that academy.@k@vS06/15/151500045. ...You understand what that means, I trust.``
``The school Granny picked just didn't suit me. That's all there is to it.``
``...Shion-san.``
``...I know, I know.@k@vS06/06/150600043. Give it a rest.``
Our grandmother's decisions, as head of the Sonozaki family, are absolute.
...Not like some frivolous rule about food being safe to eat if you pick it up within three seconds of dropping it.
What's more, the decision to confine me to a school far from the Sonozaki family... had been made from the very moment I was born.
The name I was given was 'Shion.'
My sister's name, 'Mion,' contains the character for 'oni' -- demon.@k It signifies the one who inherits the legacy of demons.
Meaning she would be the heir to the Sonozaki family and its demonic bloodline.
And my name, 'Shion,' contains the character for 'temple.'
The implication being that I would eventually be cloistered away in a temple and forgotten. That's what the name meant.
When I think about it that way, the name 'Shion' makes my stomach turn.
In the first place, the very existence of 'Shion' is an abomination to the Sonozaki family.
Two potential heirs to the main house.
...I don't need to cite historical precedents to see how that could sow the seeds of future conflict.
According to the traditions passed down through generations of the main house,@k if the heir bore twins, one was to be strangled before its first bath.@k Quite the charming custom.
Which means--
...I should be grateful just to be alive and breathing each day.
Apparently, our grandmother actually wrapped her hands around my throat right after I was born, before I'd even been bathed.
changeReason: New includes the detail "before I'd even been bathed" (産湯に浸かる前に) present in the Japanese but omitted in the old translation.

TextENGNew: ...I don't know what exchange took place in that room.
Perhaps some fearless relative stepped forward and persuaded her to stop.
Or perhaps the old woman simply happened to be in an uncommonly good mood that day.
(Then why did you put your hands around my neck in the first place?! Terrifying!)
changeReason: Old renders as a simple imperative ("don't try to strangle me") while new captures the Japanese rhetorical tone (なら手を掛けるなよな) as "why did you put your hands around my neck in the first place?!" — a notably different speech act.

TextENGNew: We're twins.@k There's not a single difference between us.
But the one who emerged from our mother's womb first was named 'Mion,' and the one who came in second -- dead last -- was named 'Shion.'
Everyone around us was desperate to tell 'Mion' and 'Shion' apart, but to us sisters, the whole effort was hilariously absurd.
We were exactly the same -- there was nothing to differentiate.
A simple swap of hats was enough to fool everyone.
Why do all the grown-ups keep trying so hard to tell us apart? -- we wondered.
Well, our little twin games couldn't go on forever.
One day, a line was drawn -- absolute and unmistakable -- separating Mion and Shion for good.
Mion would move to the Sonozaki main house to live with our grandmother and train as the heir.
And I would be confined at that academy.
changeReason: Old introduces the specific name "St. Lucia's" not present in the Japanese (例の学園 = "that academy"); new correctly leaves it unnamed.

TextENGNew: ...I have no objections, at this point, to Sis inheriting the family headship.
Sure, there was a time when I felt some resentment about it.@k But now I actually pity Mion, shackled to all those tiresome customs and traditions.
So even with me back in Okinomiya, I pose no threat to the Sonozaki main house.
But... that old hag apparently doesn't see it that way.
She treats me like some kind of ill omen -- a black cat, a crow, a cracked teacup.
changeReason: Old omits the specific ill-omen imagery (黒猫/black cat, カラス/crow, ぱっかり割れるお茶碗/cracked teacup) from the Japanese, summarizing as just "evil omen."

TextENGNew: ...That's why she wants to keep me as far away from her as possible.
Fine by me.@k ...I don't want to see her wrinkled face either.
If she declares Hinamizawa off-limits to me, I can live with that.
She can fine me ten thousand yen for every step I take inside the village for all I care.
But I want her to at least overlook Okinomiya -- the town where I grew up.
I spent my earliest years in Hinamizawa, but I'm not especially attached to it.@k My memories of the place are faint.
Okinomiya, where I spent all of elementary school, means far more to me.@k It's an unremarkable little town, but I'm rather fond of it.
``If you return to Okinomiya, sooner or later you'll cross paths with a family member.@k@vS06/15/151500048. Word will inevitably reach the main house.``
``You're worried about what happens when it reaches Granny's ears?@k@vS06/06/150600045. ......Ahaha, yeah... that could get ugly.``
``......As long as you truly understand.``
``......What is it, Kasai?@k@vS06/06/150600047. ...Are you telling me I should turn around and go back to that place?``
``Hardly.@k@vS06/15/151500051. I'd never suggest that.@k@vS06/15/151500052. ...What I'm trying to say is--``
``...That I'd better be prepared for the consequences, right...?``
``As long as you genuinely understand, and you're not just paying it lip service.``

``Well, yeah, I get it.@k@vS06/06/150600050. If things go south, you'll be held accountable too for helping me escape.@k@vS06/06/150600051. Better give that pinky finger of yours plenty of kisses while you still can, Kasai.@k@vS06/06/150600052. ...Heh heh heh heh!``

``In that case, I'd ask you to prepare yourself as well -- for being bound up in a straw mat and hurled into the Onigafuchi Swamp.@k@vS06/15/151500055. Ah, or perhaps they'll send you to the secret room in the main house's detached wing.``
I've never actually seen it myself,@k but there's supposedly a large secret chamber in the Sonozaki main house.
changeReason: Old translates 秘密部屋 (secret room/chamber) as "torture room," adding content not in the Japanese; new correctly renders it as "secret chamber."

TextENGNew: They say people who crossed the village were tortured to death there and disposed of without a trace...
changeReason: New adds "disposed of without a trace" matching 闇から闇に葬った in the Japanese, which the old translation omitted.

TextENGNew: ...Truly terrifying.
I'd rather not end up verifying those rumors firsthand.
``Yeah, yeah.@k@vS06/06/150600054. It'll work out somehow.@k@vS06/06/150600055. Ahahahaha~``
``You really are the spitting image of your mother.@k@vS06/15/151500057. That wing-it-and-hope-for-the-best attitude, especially.``
``Of course I'd take after her -- she's my actual mom.@k@vS06/06/150600057. Ahahahahaha.``
When my parents find out I've run away from school... Mom and Dad are both going to be absolutely livid...
Honestly, I'm more afraid of them than of Granny.
I'd rather not get into the details, but my father belongs to the yakuza.
He heads a sizable organization whose territory covers the greater Shishibone area, and he's also a senior executive in a larger syndicate.
changeReason: Old omits 私の忠臣 ("my loyal retainer") from the Japanese, reducing the sentence to just "So is Kasai here."

TextENGNew: Kasai here, my ever-faithful retainer, is one of his men as well.
changeReason: Old omits the temporal detail "since before my parents were even married" (結婚してなかった頃から) and the emphasis on trust being "especially deep" (一際厚い) from the Japanese.

TextENGNew: He's been friends with my father since before my parents were even married, and enjoys an especially deep level of trust.
I can't vouch for the truth of it, but apparently the gentle Kasai of today was quite the fearsome brawler in his youth.
I've never seen them, but his body is said to bear a fair number of alarming scars.

He'd managed several territories as my father's right-hand man, but after sustaining serious injuries in a turf war, he stepped back from the front lines. Now he serves as my father's advisor -- which is really just a fancy way of saying drinking buddy.
changeReason: Old omits 親父の片腕 ("father's right-hand man") from the Japanese, losing the characterization of Kasai's closeness to the father.

TextENGNew: At some point he became my minder, and before I knew it, he'd evolved into something like my personal butler.
At first, I assumed he was secretly watching me on my father's orders, and I resented his presence.
But over time, I came to believe that this man was genuinely on my side.
...Call it a woman's intuition.
...I suspect Kasai was once in love with my mother.
changeReason: Old reduces the Japanese meaning (仮定を持って見てみると/viewing through that hypothesis) to vague "from the way he acts," losing the investigative framing of the narrator's reasoning.

TextENGNew: I think there was a bit of a love triangle between him and my father.
In the end, my father beat him to the punch.
But Kasai couldn't bring himself to leave my mother's orbit entirely, so he stayed on as a lifelong friend.
Viewing Kasai through that lens,@k quite a few things suddenly clicked into place.
Through me, he's seeing the shadow of my mother as she was in her youth... that's how it felt.
The way he'd say, at every opportunity, how much I resembled her -- that especially gave off that impression.
I did find him a bit unsettling at first, but Kasai bears me no ill will.
...Once I let my guard down, I discovered he was actually a fun, witty man.
So when it came to finding someone who'd help me escape from a confinement ordered by the Sonozaki main house, Kasai was the only possible choice.
Early on in my planning, I'd gotten in touch with Kasai and coordinated the escape with him.@k He's my co-conspirator in this escapade -- accomplice, co-star, call him what you will.
...Knowing all that, it was only natural for Kasai to worry about what would happen after my return to Okinomiya.
Given the tangled web of obligations to my father and the main house... in the worst case, having a finger taken wasn't beyond the realm of possibility.
Ahaha -- and the bit about me being rolled up in a mat and thrown into the swamp might not be so far-fetched either.
Even so, there was only one place I was going back to.
Nowhere but Okinomiya.
Because that was my home.
...You could call this attachment a kind of homesickness -- a simple, honest yearning for home.
I had no interest in trying to dress it up as anything else.
``Realistically, what's your plan?@k@vS06/15/151500059. The Sonozaki family has eyes and ears everywhere.``
``Well, I'll keep a low profile at first.@k@vS06/06/150600059. And if someone spots me, I can always insist I'm Mion.@k@vS06/06/150600060. Ahaha!``
``And after that?``
``Once things cool down a bit, I'll gradually come clean to the relatives closest to me.@k@vS06/06/150600062. I do have a decent number of people who'd take my side, you know.``
``...Back when I had that huge blowup with Dad, I bounced around from place to place with different people sheltering me.``

``I see.@k@vS06/15/151500062. So the plan is to build up a base of allies and establish facts on the ground, and ultimately coax the main house into turning a blind eye.@k@vS06/15/151500063. ...Whether that'll actually work is another matter.``

``It'll work!@k@vS06/06/150600065. Plenty of people opposed sending me to that school in the first place.@k@vS06/06/150600066. That decision was born from nothing but the old hag's paranoid distrust of me.@k@vS06/06/150600067. ...Don't you think so, Kasai?``
``...Yes, well.@k@vS06/15/151500065. ...I did think that decision went too far.``
``Granny overthinks everything until she can't think straight anymore!@k@vS06/06/150600069. She's been wound tight since the Dam War, and I bet she still hasn't come down from that high. Honestly!``
Kasai couldn't quite bring himself to nod along, but his rueful smile said enough.
changeReason: Old omits the characterization of the narrator's sulky pout (むぅ…っと) and Kasai holding back laughter (堪えきれなくなった) from the Japanese, simplifying the scene.

TextENGNew: In the end, I stayed wrapped in my blanket and never did fall asleep.
Despite telling him to be quiet, I'd been chatting with Kasai the entire time.
...Eventually, talked out and weary, I was just beginning to appreciate the lulling monotony of the highway's rhythm when Kasai spoke up unexpectedly.
I was too drowsy by that point and decided to ignore him.
...I was mildly curious about what he wanted me to see, but not enough to open my eyes.
Still, I cracked my eyelids open just slightly, and a sign hurtled into my field of vision at tremendous speed before vanishing behind us.
That must have been what Kasai wanted to show me.
On the sign, alongside an illustration of azaleas -- the city's flower -- were the words:
'Welcome to Shishibone City.'
Home was just around the corner.
